stem: Which of the following best describes encapsulation in object-oriented programming?

options.label: A
options.text: Hiding internal implementation details and exposing state only through public methods

options.label: B
options.text: Allowing all fields and methods to be accessed from any other class

options.label: C
options.text: Breaking a large method into smaller helper methods

options.label: D
options.text: Giving multiple methods the same name with different parameter lists

explanation: Encapsulation means keeping internal data private and providing controlled access through methods. B describes the opposite of encapsulation. C is method decomposition. D is method overloading.

stem: A class `BankAccount` needs to store an account balance as a decimal number. Which of the following is the most appropriate field declaration, following standard Java class-design conventions?

options.label: A
options.text: `public double balance;`

options.label: B
options.text: `private double balance;`

options.label: C
options.text: `public static double balance;`

options.label: D
options.text: `private static double balance;`

explanation: Instance variables should be `private` to enforce encapsulation, and each `BankAccount` object should have its own balance (not `static`). A exposes the field publicly, violating encapsulation. C and D make the field `static`, so all accounts would share one balance.

options.label: A
options.text: `Point@` followed by a hexadecimal memory address

options.label: D
options.text: A compile error occurs because `p` is not a `String`.

explanation: `System.out.println` automatically calls `toString()` on any object. Because `toString()` is overridden to return `"(" + x + ", " + y + ")"`, the output is `(3, 7)`. A would occur only if `toString()` were not overridden (the default `Object.toString()` produces the class-name-and-hash form).

options.label: C
options.text: `radius`

options.label: D
options.text: A compile error occurs.

explanation: Inside the constructor, `radius = radius` assigns the parameter to itself — the instance variable `radius` is never set and retains its default value of `0.0`. The fix is `this.radius = radius`. No compile error occurs; this is a silent logical bug caused by parameter shadowing.

options.label: A
options.text: `a.getId()` is `1`, `Counter.getCount()` is `1`

options.label: B
options.text: `a.getId()` is `1`, `Counter.getCount()` is `3`

options.label: C
options.text: `a.getId()` is `3`, `Counter.getCount()` is `3`

options.label: D
options.text: `a.getId()` is `0`, `Counter.getCount()` is `3`

explanation: `count` is static and shared across all instances. Object `a` was constructed first: `count` incremented to 1 and `a.id` was set to 1. After all three constructors run, `count` is 3. C is wrong because `a` was the first Counter created. D is wrong because `id` is set to the post-increment value of `count`, not its pre-increment value.

`b` inside `change` is a copy of the reference to the same object as `myBox`. `b.setValue(50)` mutates the shared object so its `value` becomes 50. The subsequent `b = new Box(99)` reassigns only the local parameter `b`; it does not affect `myBox`. So `myBox.getValue()` returns 50.

options.label: C
options.text: `null`, `null`, `null`

options.label: D
options.text: A compile error occurs because no constructor is explicitly defined.

explanation: Java initializes primitive instance variables to their type defaults: `int` → `0`, `double` → `0.0`, `boolean` → `false`. A is wrong because `double` defaults to `0.0`, not `0`. C applies to reference types, not primitives. D is wrong because Java provides a default no-arg constructor automatically when no constructor is written.

options.label: A
options.text: `count` should not be declared `static` because it belongs to each individual instance.

options.label: B
options.text: The constructor should be declared `static` to match the static field.

options.label: C
options.text: Line X attempts to access the instance variable `name` from a `static` method, which is not allowed.

options.label: D
options.text: Line X should use `this.name` instead of `name` to resolve the ambiguity.

explanation: Static methods have no implicit `this` reference and cannot directly access instance variables. `name` is an instance variable, so referencing it in `getInfo()` produces a compile error ('non-static variable name cannot be referenced from a static context'). A is incorrect — using `static` for a class-wide counter is appropriate. D is wrong because `this` is also unavailable in a static method.

options.label: A
options.text: `4x6 area=24`

options.label: B
options.text: `Rectangle area=24`

options.label: C
options.text: `width x height area=24`

options.label: D
options.text: `4 x 6 area=24`

explanation: String concatenation with an object automatically calls `toString()`. The overridden `toString()` returns `"4x6"` (no spaces around `"x"`), and `r.area()` returns `4 * 6 = 24`. B would occur if `toString()` were not overridden. C and D introduce spaces that the `toString()` implementation does not produce.

options.label: A
options.text: `r` contains only `"Alice"` because `getNames()` returns a copy of the internal list.

options.label: B
options.text: `r` contains both `"Alice"` and `"Bob"` because `getNames()` returns a reference to the internal list.

options.label: C
options.text: `r` contains only `"Bob"` because `list.add` replaces the original list.

options.label: D
options.text: A compile error occurs because `private` fields cannot be returned from a method.

explanation: `getNames()` returns a reference to the actual `ArrayList` stored in `r`, not a defensive copy. When `list.add("Bob")` is called, it modifies the same object that `r.names` points to. To protect internal state, `getNames()` should return `new ArrayList<String>(names)`. D is wrong — access modifiers control external direct access to a field, not what a method is allowed to return.

options.label: A
options.text: The code compiles and runs; `x` is created with `label` set to `null` and `quantity` set to `0`, and `bolt` is printed.

options.label: B
options.text: A compile error occurs because `new Item()` calls a no-argument constructor that does not exist.

options.label: C
options.text: A `NullPointerException` is thrown at runtime when `new Item()` is executed.

options.label: D
options.text: The code compiles and `bolt` is printed; Java always provides a default no-argument constructor.

explanation: Once a programmer writes any constructor, Java no longer provides the default no-argument constructor. Since `Item` defines a two-parameter constructor, `new Item()` has no matching signature and causes a compile error. D is false — the default constructor is only available when NO constructors are defined. The error is detected at compile time, not runtime, so C is also wrong.

options.label: A
options.text: `true`

options.label: B
options.text: `false`

options.label: C
options.text: A compile error occurs because `other.degrees` accesses a `private` field of another object.

options.label: D
options.text: A runtime error occurs because `other` could be `null`.

explanation: `t1.degrees` is 75.0 and `t2.degrees` is 68.0, so `75.0 > 68.0` evaluates to `true`. C is a common misconception — a method may access `private` fields of any instance of the same class; `other.degrees` is valid because `other` is also a `Temperature`. D is incorrect; `t2` is a properly constructed object and the precondition assumes non-null arguments.

stem: Which of the following best describes encapsulation in object-oriented design?

options.label: A
options.text: Breaking a large method into smaller, named helper methods so each does one job

options.label: B
options.text: Naming a method so callers can use it without knowing its implementation

options.label: C
options.text: Keeping instance data `private` and exposing it only through public methods

options.label: D
options.text: Allowing any class to read and modify another class's variables directly

explanation: Encapsulation means hiding internal state behind private access modifiers and providing controlled access via public methods. A describes method decomposition; B describes procedural abstraction; D is the opposite of encapsulation.

stem: A class `BankAccount` needs an instance variable to store the account balance as a decimal value. Each `BankAccount` object should maintain its own independent balance. Which of the following is the most appropriate declaration for this field?

options.label: A
options.text: `public double balance;`

options.label: B
options.text: `private double balance;`

options.label: C
options.text: `private static double balance;`

options.label: D
options.text: `public static double balance;`

explanation: Instance variables should be private (encapsulation) and non-static (each object holds its own copy). A violates encapsulation by exposing the field publicly. C and D use `static`, which would create one balance value shared across all BankAccount objects rather than a per-object value.

Inside the constructor, `width = width` assigns the parameter back to itself due to shadowing — the instance variable `this.width` is never updated and retains its default value of 0. The same bug affects `height`. Both getters therefore return 0. Option A would be correct if `this.width = width` had been written instead.

Each constructor call increments the shared static `count` before assigning it to the new object's `id`. After three constructions, `count` is 3. Object `a` was the first constructed, so its `id` was set when `count` first reached 1; `a.getId()` returns 1. `Counter.getCount()` returns the current class-level value of 3. D is a common mistake from confusing `a`'s id with the final count value.

Inside `shift`, `x = this.x + x` computes 3+2=5 and writes the result into the local parameter `x`, not into the instance variable `this.x`. Neither `this.x` nor `this.y` is ever modified, so the getters still return the constructor values 3 and 4. Option A would be correct if the method body read `this.x = this.x + x;` and `this.y = this.y + y;` instead.

stem: Which of the following best describes **encapsulation** as it applies to a Java class?

options.label: A
options.text: Breaking a large method into smaller helper methods to reduce complexity

options.label: B
options.text: Keeping instance variables `private` and exposing them only through public methods

options.label: C
options.text: Giving a process a name so callers can use it without knowing its implementation

options.label: D
options.text: Declaring all methods as `static` so they belong to the class rather than to any object

explanation: Encapsulation means hiding internal data by declaring instance variables `private` and controlling access through public methods. Option A describes method decomposition; option C describes procedural abstraction; option D describes static methods, which are unrelated to encapsulation.

stem: A `Car` class needs an instance variable to store the number of miles driven for each individual car, hidden from outside code. Which of the following is the most appropriate field declaration?

options.label: A
options.text: `public int milesDriven;`

options.label: B
options.text: `private int milesDriven;`

options.label: C
options.text: `private static int milesDriven;`

options.label: D
options.text: `public static int milesDriven;`

explanation: Instance variables should be `private` (to hide them from outside code) and non-`static` (each Car needs its own milesDriven, not one value shared by all Cars). Option A uses `public`, violating encapsulation. Options C and D use `static`, which would create a single shared value across all Car objects.

options.label: A
options.text: `Point@` followed by a hexadecimal hash code

explanation: Because `toString()` is overridden, `println(p)` automatically calls it and prints `(4, 7)`. Option A would appear only if `toString` were not overridden and the default `Object.toString()` ran. Option B reverses the order of the constructor arguments. Option D does not match the literal string produced by the overridden method.

options.label: D
options.text: A compile-time error occurs

explanation: The statement `brightness = brightness` assigns the constructor parameter to itself — the instance variable `brightness` is never written, so it retains the default `int` value of `0`. Option A would be correct if `this.brightness = brightness` were used instead. Option C reflects a misconception that `int` fields default to `1` (they default to `0`). Option D is wrong because shadowing is legal Java and compiles without error.

`nextId` is a static variable shared by all instances. Creating t1 sets its `id` to 1 and increments `nextId` to 2. Creating t2 sets its `id` to 2 and increments `nextId` to 3. Creating t3 sets its `id` to 3 and increments `nextId` to 4. `t2.getId()` returns the value stored in t2's `id` field, which is `2`. Option D confuses `nextId`'s final value (4) with t2's stored `id`.

stem: Which of the following statements about `static` methods in a Java class is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: A `static` method can directly access instance variables of the class without an object reference.

options.label: B
options.text: A `static` method can use the `this` keyword to refer to the current object.

options.label: C
options.text: A `static` method belongs to the class itself and not to any individual object.

options.label: D
options.text: A `static` method must be declared `private` to prevent access from outside the class.

explanation: A static method belongs to the class rather than to any instance, so it can be called without creating an object (e.g., `Math.abs(-3)`). It has no implicit current object, so it cannot use `this` (ruling out option B) and cannot directly access instance variables (ruling out option A). Static methods are commonly `public`, so option D is incorrect.

options.label: A
options.text: `15 true`

options.label: B
options.text: `15 false`

options.label: C
options.text: `10 false`

options.label: D
options.text: `ScoreCard@15`

explanation: The constructor sets `score` to 10, `addPoints` changes it to 15, and `complete` keeps its default value `false`. Option A incorrectly assumes `finish` was called, C ignores the mutator call, and D ignores the defined `toString` method.

stem: A `Ticket` object should store a section name and a seat number. Outside code should not access these values directly. Which of the following is the most appropriate declaration for the instance variables?

options.label: A
B
C
D

explanation: Instance variables should normally be `private` so the class controls access through methods. B exposes the data directly, C makes the data shared by all `Ticket` objects, and D does not explicitly use private access.
